interrogator_dossier:
# InnovAge Holding Corp (INNV) — Forensic Interrogation Dossier

*Source transcripts: FY25-Q3 (2025-05-06), FY25-Q4 (2025-09-09), FY26-Q1 (2025-11-04), FY26-Q2 (2026-02-03). Financials from screener scan (FY ending June; TTM as of 2026-03-31). Live web verification of FY26-Q3 (reported 2025-05-05 [sic: 2026-05-05]) appended where the transcript bundle ends.*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

InnovAge is the largest U.S. PACE (Program of All-inclusive Care for the Elderly) operator — a fully-capitated, payer-and-provider model serving ~8,000 frail dual-eligible seniors across 20 centers in 6 states. The transcript arc is one of the cleaner **operational-turnaround narratives** in the screen, and the story tightens rather than drifts across the four calls.

- **FY25-Q3 (May 2025) — "stabilization → transformation."** Blair frames the company as having "shifted from operational stabilization to enterprise transformation." The headline metric is the climb off the bottom: adjusted EBITDA $10.8M (4.9% margin), "more than 3.5 times" the $3M of the prior-year quarter. Census ~7,530 (+10% YoY). The emphasis is on proving the medical-cost-control thesis through a "quad-demic" flu season (external provider cost held flat at $108M; PMPM *declined* QoQ).
- **FY25-Q4 / full-year (Sept 2025) — "a year of delivery."** The arc widens to a scorecard against the Feb-2024 Investor Day commitments: FY25 revenue $853.7M (+~12%), adjusted EBITDA $34.5M (above the high end of the $31M guide), margin ~doubled from 2.2% to ~4%. The forward story is introduced here — **FY26 guidance of $900–950M revenue and $56–65M adjusted EBITDA**, with an explicit medium-term **8–9% EBITDA-margin target** "over the next few years." Growth drivers added: Orlando Health and Tampa General JVs, plus a policy-expansion option ("Medicare-only PACE").
- **FY26-Q1 (Nov 2025) — "first positive net income since 2021."** Revenue $236.1M (+15%), adjusted EBITDA $17.6M (doubled YoY), census all-time-high 7,890. The narrative pivots from "are we profitable" to "how durable is the margin," with in-sourcing (pharmacy, hospice) reframed from a cost headwind into a structural margin lever. Notably this quarter also carries a *rash of management turnover* (COO Scarbrough out; new CMO, CAO, CGO).
- **FY26-Q2 (Feb 2026) — "8–9% target hit, two years early."** Revenue $239.7M, adjusted EBITDA $22.2M, **9.2% margin** — Blair explicitly: "This quarter, for the first time, we achieved that target." Net income $11.8M. First-half EBITDA ($39.8M) already exceeded *all of FY25* ($34.5M). The forward narrative shifts again — from "hit the target" to "the next 50%" of clinical-value capture (ordering-pattern variation, the new CMO Taheri's mandate).

**Net arc:** a credible, sequentially-compounding margin story that has consistently *raised* the bar — from "can we be EBITDA-positive" (FY25-Q3) to "we beat the multi-year target two years early" (FY26-Q2). The growth drivers introduced (JVs, in-sourcing, spans-and-layers) have shown up in the numbers rather than being quietly dropped.

---

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

The scan's fiscal-year-annual block (through FY25, June year-end) lags the transcript momentum badly because the inflection is *intra-FY26*. The TTM block (as of 2026-03-31: TTM FCF $39M, TTM diluted EPS -$0.30) and the transcripts together tell the real story.

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: RISING (and earned).**

- **FY25-Q3:** measured, still-defensive — "we know what we need to do and we're doing it, steadily and consistently"; CFO Adams *refuses* to commit on Q4 ("not going to give any sort of indication … there tends to be a fair amount of variability"). Confidence present but hedged.
- **FY25-Q4:** distinctly more offensive — a litany of "we committed … and we delivered" (margins, clinical outcomes, revenue, operating leverage, EBITDA, compliance). First time they set a multi-year 8–9% target out loud.
- **FY26-Q1:** confident, with a reflective CEO tone ("As I approach my fourth anniversary as CEO …"), and willing to absorb management departures without flinching ("transitions … have not disrupted our momentum").
- **FY26-Q2:** peak confidence — claiming the multi-year target hit "for the first time," and immediately re-aiming at "the next 50%" of value capture. This is offensive, growth/quality language, not defensive cost-cutting language.

The shift is **from defensive (cost discipline, compliance clean-up, stabilization) toward offensive (margin target beaten, multi-year value-capture roadmap)** — the healthy direction. Guidance has become *more* specific over time, not less (FY26 census/member-month/revenue/EBITDA/de-novo all quantified in Q4).

---

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: HIGH.** This is the strongest single pillar of the name.

- FY25 adjusted EBITDA came in at **$34.5M, above the high end of the $31M guide** (Q4 call). Delivered > guided.
- FY26 was guided at $56–65M EBITDA (Q4, Sept 2025). By H1 FY26 they had already booked $39.8M — tracking to the **upper end or above**.
- The 8–9% medium-term margin target (set Feb 2024 Investor Day, reiterated Sept 2025) was **hit in FY26-Q2, ~2 years inside the 2–4-year window.**
- The scan's beat-rate is **3/6 quarters (50%)** on consensus EPS — but that is the *GAAP-EPS* line, which legacy litigation and de-novo drag distort. On the metric management actually guides (adjusted EBITDA / revenue), the delivery record is clean.

**Sandbagging pattern:** mild and constructive. Adams repeatedly under-commits intra-quarter ("we're happy to let guidance stand," "variability in true-up numbers") and then the company beats. The pattern is conservative-guide-then-beat, which is the credible kind.

**LIVE CONFIRMATION (web, post-bundle):** FY26-Q3 (reported May 5, 2026) printed revenue **$251.9M (+15.5%)** and adjusted EBITDA **$30.5M (12.1% margin)**, and management **raised FY26 guidance again** to $950–975M revenue / **$85–90M adjusted EBITDA** — i.e., the EBITDA guide has roughly *doubled* from the original $56–65M over three quarters. The guidance-credibility thesis is not just intact, it has strengthened since the last transcript.

- **De-novo losses and time-to-breakeven** (Goldman's Perse, FY25-Q3): pushed on the cash drag from Florida/Crenshaw centers. # InnovAge Holding Corp (INNV) — Forensic Interrogation Dossier

*Source transcripts: FY25-Q3 (2025-05-06), FY25-Q4 (2025-09-09), FY26-Q1 (2025-11-04), FY26-Q2 (2026-02-03). Financials from screener scan (FY ending June; TTM as of 2026-03-31). Live web verification of FY26-Q3 (reported 2025-05-05 [sic: 2026-05-05]) appended where the transcript bundle ends.*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

InnovAge is the largest U.S. PACE (Program of All-inclusive Care for the Elderly) operator — a fully-capitated, payer-and-provider model serving ~8,000 frail dual-eligible seniors across 20 centers in 6 states. The transcript arc is one of the cleaner **operational-turnaround narratives** in the screen, and the story tightens rather than drifts across the four calls.

- **FY25-Q3 (May 2025) — "stabilization → transformation."** Blair frames the company as having "shifted from operational stabilization to enterprise transformation." The headline metric is the climb off the bottom: adjusted EBITDA $10.8M (4.9% margin), "more than 3.5 times" the $3M of the prior-year quarter. Census ~7,530 (+10% YoY). The emphasis is on proving the medical-cost-control thesis through a "quad-demic" flu season (external provider cost held flat at $108M; PMPM *declined* QoQ).
- **FY25-Q4 / full-year (Sept 2025) — "a year of delivery."** The arc widens to a scorecard against the Feb-2024 Investor Day commitments: FY25 revenue $853.7M (+~12%), adjusted EBITDA $34.5M (above the high end of the $31M guide), margin ~doubled from 2.2% to ~4%. The forward story is introduced here — **FY26 guidance of $900–950M revenue and $56–65M adjusted EBITDA**, with an explicit medium-term **8–9% EBITDA-margin target** "over the next few years." Growth drivers added: Orlando Health and Tampa General JVs, plus a policy-expansion option ("Medicare-only PACE").
- **FY26-Q1 (Nov 2025) — "first positive net income since 2021."** Revenue $236.1M (+15%), adjusted EBITDA $17.6M (doubled YoY), census all-time-high 7,890. The narrative pivots from "are we profitable" to "how durable is the margin," with in-sourcing (pharmacy, hospice) reframed from a cost headwind into a structural margin lever. Notably this quarter also carries a *rash of management turnover* (COO Scarbrough out; new CMO, CAO, CGO).
- **FY26-Q2 (Feb 2026) — "8–9% target hit, two years early."** Revenue $239.7M, adjusted EBITDA $22.2M, **9.2% margin** — Blair explicitly: "This quarter, for the first time, we achieved that target." Net income $11.8M. First-half EBITDA ($39.8M) already exceeded *all of FY25* ($34.5M). The forward narrative shifts again — from "hit the target" to "the next 50%" of clinical-value capture (ordering-pattern variation, the new CMO Taheri's mandate).

**Net arc:** a credible, sequentially-compounding margin story that has consistently *raised* the bar — from "can we be EBITDA-positive" (FY25-Q3) to "we beat the multi-year target two years early" (FY26-Q2). The growth drivers introduced (JVs, in-sourcing, spans-and-layers) have shown up in the numbers rather than being quietly dropped.

---

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

The scan's fiscal-year-annual block (through FY25, June year-end) lags the transcript momentum badly because the inflection is *intra-FY26*. The TTM block (as of 2026-03-31: TTM FCF $39M, TTM diluted EPS -$0.30) and the transcripts together tell the real story.

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: RISING (and earned).**

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: HIGH.** This is the strongest single pillar of the name.

- FY25 adjusted EBITDA came in at **$34.5M, above the high end of the $31M guide** (Q4 call). Delivered > guided.

**Sandbagging pattern:** mild and constructive. Adams repeatedly under-commits intra-quarter ("we're happy to let guidance stand," "variability in true-up numbers") and then the company beats. The pattern is conservative-guide-then-beat, which is the credible kind.

**LIVE CONFIRMATION (web, post-bundle):** FY26-Q3 (reported May 5, 2026) printed revenue **$251.9M (+15.5%)** and adjusted EBITDA **$30.5M (12.1% margin)**, and management **raised FY26 guidance again** to $950–975M revenue / **$85–90M adjusted EBITDA** — i.e., the EBITDA guide has roughly *doubled* from the original $56–65M over three quarters. consensus_delta: For a deep_value signal the test is: 'is the street pricing terminal decline into stable/growing earnings power?' Here the answer is NO — the street is NOT looking the wrong way in the bearish direction; if anything it is more cautious than the price (consensus target $6.80 = -27% vs the ~$9.28 print). The CY2027 CMS rate finalization (V28 50/50 blended risk model) is the binding policy clock and it skews negative. The only POSITIVE forcing function would be a PE-sponsor-driven corporate event — a take-private, strategic sale, or large secondary — which is unannounced, non-binding, and entirely at Apax/WCAS's discretion: a SOFT, single-binary, third-party-controlled lever. Under the current regime (which rewards hard-dated catalysts in-window and penalizes past/out-of-window ones), INNV's load-bearing catalyst is in the PAST.
valley_of_death: No solvency valley — net cash $45M, Altman-Z 3.9, no maturity wall. The near-term (3-9mo) risk is a RATE/GUIDANCE valley, not a cash valley: (1) the September FY27 guide is the gating event — management deflected all 2027 specifics ('we're in the meat of the budgeting process,' Q3-26) and a flat-to-down EBITDA/margin guide into the V28 50/50 blended risk-model transition and ~1.5-2% PACE rate would puncture the 'durable momentum' narrative; (2) de novo Florida losses are GROWING ($13.4-15.4M FY26), a persistent FCF drag with no dated break-even; (3) OBBBA/Medicaid state fiscal pressure + redeterminations can reverse the very Medicaid-rate tailwind that drove the FY26 beat; (4) FCF is thin (3.1% margin, P/FCF 47x) so any reserve/true-up volatility (which CFO Adams repeatedly warns is 'lumpy') hits a richly-priced stock hard. positioning_washout: Limited mechanical-washout dynamic in the classic sense (no dividend to cut, not a yield/passive holder base). The dominant positioning fact is the OPPOSITE — Apax+WCAS hold ~70-83% combined, institutional ownership >90%, leaving a thin true float. This cuts two ways: (a) it creates a permanent forced-seller OVERHANG — the PE sponsors are ~5 years post-IPO and will eventually monetize via secondary/block sales (the relevant 'washout' here is sponsor distribution into a thin float, which would cap upside and pressure the stock), and (b) the thin float makes the stock momentum-driven and gap-prone in both directions. There is no yield-investor capitulation to harvest; the asymmetric special-sit washout the framework looks for is absent. If anything, the position to fear is being the marginal buyer ahead of an eventual sponsor exit.
moderator_conclusion: PASS (C), conviction 2. INNV is a genuinely good, well-executed turnaround story — the Interrogator scores credibility 4, the balance sheet is net-cash, and the FY26 adj. interrogator_dossier:
# InnovAge Holding Corp. (INNV) — Forensic Dossier

*Interrogator dossier. Five consecutive earnings calls: FY25 Q3 (2025-05-06), FY25 Q4 / full year (2025-09-09), FY26 Q1 (2025-11-04), FY26 Q2 (2026-02-03), FY26 Q3 (2026-05-05). InnovAge is the largest PACE (Program of All-inclusive Care for the Elderly) operator in the U.S. — ~8,050 frail dual-eligible participants, 20 centers, 6 states, full-risk capitated Medicare+Medicaid revenue. The company is still living down a 2021–2022 CMS/state enrollment SANCTION (Sacramento, Colorado) that froze new enrollment and nearly broke the equity.*

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The arc across these five calls is one of a **textbook operational turnaround completing faster than management itself guided** — from "stabilization" to "transformation" to "we pulled three years of margin into two."

- **FY25 Q3 (May-25):** The framing is *"shifted from operational stabilization to enterprise transformation."* Management is cautious — reaffirming (not raising) FY25 guidance, adj. EBITDA only $10.8M for the quarter (4.9% margin), de novo losses a visible drag. The story is "we have momentum but we're early." CMO Dr. Rich Feifer departs — the first of a long string of C-suite exits.
- **FY25 Q4 (Sep-25):** The year is reframed as *"a year of delivery… we made clear commitments, and we followed through."* FY25 adj. EBITDA of $34.5M lands **above the high end** of the $31M guide. The forward story expands: a Medicare-only PACE pathway, Tampa General JV alongside Orlando Health, an explicit 8–9% EBITDA-margin target "over the next few years."
- **FY26 Q1 (Nov-25):** **First positive net income since 2021.** The narrative pivots to "resilient model in a hostile VBC environment" — PACE is differentiated because it is payer + provider in one. But this is also the **leadership-churn quarter**: President/COO Michael Scarbrough out "for personal reasons," new CMO (Taheri), new CAO (Delk), expanded CGO (Huray), plus a "spans and layers" headcount reduction.
- **FY26 Q2 (Feb-26):** **The 8–9% target is hit early — 9.2% adj. EBITDA margin, net income $11.8M.** H1 EBITDA ($39.8M) already exceeds all of FY25. Management starts pre-managing the 2027 rate story (CMS CY2027 MA advance notice, 50/50 blended V28 risk model).
- **FY26 Q3 (May-26):** Guidance raised **again** — revenue to $950–975M, adj. EBITDA to **$85–90M** (from $34.5M a year prior). Crucially, the CEO concedes the upside is partly *in-year luck*: *"several in-year factors that came in more favorably than we expected, including better-than-expected Medicaid rates and favorable Medicare risk scores."* The forward emphasis shifts from margin expansion to **growth reinvestment** and **AI** — *"investing in growth while maintaining a consistent margin is probably more of a priority than expanding margins at this point."*

**Drivers introduced:** Medicare-only pathway (advocacy, not revenue), hospital JVs (Orlando, Tampa General), in-sourcing (pharmacy, hospice, comfort care), Epic/Oracle/Salesforce tech stack, and — most recently — **AI for scheduling/coordination and clinical synthesis**. **Drivers quietly de-emphasized:** the de novo Florida centers are now described as "still maturing" with FY26 de novo losses of $13.4–15.4M (a real, growing drag), and standalone margin expansion is explicitly subordinated to reinvestment.

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

The annual trajectory in the scan tells a longer, uglier story than the five-quarter call window — but the **TTM/latest-quarter override and the live FY26 guide confirm a genuine, sharp inflection in the most recent four quarters.**

| Management claim | Quarter | Financial evidence | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Revenue grew at >10% CAGR FY23→FY25" | Q4-25 | Rev 688M→764M→854M (+11.8% YoY FY25); 3-yr CAGR +6.9% | **TRUE.** Top line is real and accelerating; FY26 Q3 rev $252M is +15% YoY. |
| "Adj. EBITDA more than doubled… 9.2% margin, hit our 8-9% target" | Q2-26 | FY25 adj. EBITDA $34.5M (~4%) → FY26 guide $85-90M (~9%) | **TRUE on adj. EBITDA**, but note the gap to GAAP. |
| "Positive net income… first since 2021" | Q1-26 | Annual NI: -44M / -7M / -41M / -21M / **-30M (FY25)**. TTM diluted EPS still **-$0.30**; net margin **-3.6%** | **MIXED / the key tension.** Quarterly GAAP profitability has appeared (Q2-26 NI $11.8M), but on a TTM/full-year basis the company is **still GAAP-loss-making** (ROE -10.2%, ROIC -1.5%). "Adjusted" EBITDA is doing heavy lifting vs. GAAP. |
| "Disciplined medical cost management… PMPM declined" | Q3-25, Q1-26, Q3-26 | Gross margin 68.5% and *expanding*; external provider cost essentially flat Q/Q through a "quad-demic" | **TRUE and the strongest evidence.** This is the operational heart of the turnaround and it shows in the gross margin line. |
| "Cleaned up the balance sheet… divested non-core" | Q1-26 | **Net CASH $45M** (cash $139M > debt $94M, recomputed 2026-03-31); Altman-Z 3.9 (safe) | **TRUE.** Balance sheet is genuinely de-risked — no maturity wall, no solvency flag. A real SoP add-back. |
| Census growth | all | 7,530 → 7,740 → 7,890 → ~8,050; ~10% FY25, decelerating to low-single-digit FY26 guide | **TRUE but slowing** — and management *chose* the slowdown (redetermination cleanup trades census for EBITDA, per Q4-25 Q&A). |
| Consensus fwd EPS growth +280% | scan | Off a near-zero/negative base | **Optically huge, base-effect-driven.** Real but not a clean read. |

**Where the story is richer than the numbers:** GAAP earnings power. The narrative ("profitable, hit our targets") leans on *adjusted* EBITDA while the company posted a **-$30M GAAP net loss in FY25** and TTM EPS remains **-$0.30**. P/FCF is **47x** on 3.1% FCF margin — this is **not** statistically cheap on trailing cash earnings. The bull case requires believing the FY26 run-rate ($85-90M adj. EBITDA) is the true earnings base, not the trailing loss.

**Where the story is poorer than the numbers deserve:** The forward EBITDA inflection is steeper than the modest census growth would suggest — it is margin/rate/risk-score-driven, which is exactly the part management flagged as **partly transitory** ("in-year factors").

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY — **RISING, then subtly HEDGING**

| Quarter | Posture | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| Q3-25 | Cautious-constructive | "met our expectations… reaffirming guidance"; declined to guide Q4 within the range ("happy to let guidance stand"). |
| Q4-25 | Confident | "a year of delivery… we followed through… exceeded expectations." Lays out 8-9% target. |
| Q1-26 | Confident, slightly defensive on people | "first positive net income since 2021" — but a paragraph spent reassuring on COO departure / "spans and layers." |
| Q2-26 | Peak confidence | "for the first time, we achieved that target… 9.2%." |
| Q3-26 | Confident-but-hedging | Raises guidance AND volunteers the upside is partly luck: *"in-year factors that came in more favorably than we expected"*; *"thoughtful about our assumptions as we look ahead to fiscal 2027."* |

The shift in Q3-26 is the tell: management is **proactively de-risking the 2027 setup** — moving the emphasis from "expanding margins" to "investing in growth while maintaining a consistent margin." That is the language of a team that does **not** expect the margin tailwind to repeat. Offensive→defensive on the *rate* axis specifically.

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY — **HIGH**

This is the dossier's strongest green. The serial-raise track record is excellent and **verifiable against later prints**:

- FY25 adj. EBITDA guide ~$31M → **delivered $34.5M (beat the high end).**
- FY26 adj. EBITDA: initial **$56-65M** (Sep-25) → raised to ~**$70-75M** (Feb-26) → raised to **$85-90M** (May-26). Three raises in three quarters, each followed by a beat. The endpoint is **~2.5x the original guide.**
- The scan's "beat 3/6 qtrs (50%)" understates this — it is polluted by the pre-turnaround quarters; the **last four quarters are a clean sweep of raises.**

There IS a mild **sandbagging/lumpiness pattern** Ben Adams admits to (Q3-25: "fair amount of variability in those [Q4 true-up] numbers"; Q1-26: pharmacy in-sourcing moved costs around the P&L "geography"). Reliability: **HIGH** on adjusted EBITDA delivery. The one asterisk — they themselves now flag that the FY26 beat rode rate/risk-score luck, so the *durability* of the level (not the credibility of the guide) is the open question.

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **De novo / Florida losses (Goldman, Q3-25):** Jamie Perse pushed on the path to break-even; management gave time but no hard date ("we'll put out some guidance"). FY26 de novo loss guide then *grew* to $13.4-15.4M — a real, persistent drag the bulls under-weight.
- **Cost-of-care step-ups & P&L "geography" (Perse Q3-25; Gillmore Q1-26):** Analysts repeatedly flagged that cost-of-care jumped (+17-19.7% Q/Q at points) faster than member-months. Management's answer — "it's in-sourcing, look at external provider + cost-of-care *together*" — is plausible but means the **clean organic cost trend is hard to audit from outside.** This is the recurring soft spot.
- **2027 rate environment (Haase, Rossi/JPM, Q3-26):** The newest and most important pressure point. Analysts are probing acuity mix, risk scores, and the growth-vs-margin trade-off into a softer rate year. Management deflected specifics to the September guide ("we're in the meat of the budgeting process").
- **The bigger thing analysts are NOT pressing hard on:** the **PE-sponsor control overhang** (Apax+WCAS ~70%+) and what an eventual exit/secondary does to the float. Largely absent from these calls.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🟢 **Serial guidance raises, each beaten** — FY26 adj. EBITDA guide tripled trajectory ($34.5M→$85-90M). Best-in-class credibility (§4).
🟢 **Gross margin 68.5% and expanding; medical cost discipline through a "quad-demic"** — the operational core is real (§2).
🟢 **Net cash $45M, Altman-Z 3.9, no maturity wall** — solvency is not a question; balance sheet cleaned up.
🟢 **Insider open-market buying** — 12 buys (181,653 sh) vs 13 sells (70,005 sh); net *bought* on a share-count basis. Genuine (not grant-inflated) conviction.
🟢 **Largest PACE operator + MACPAC validation** — scale and a regulatory tailwind narrative (Medicare-only pathway).

🔴 **Still GAAP-unprofitable on a TTM/annual basis** — TTM EPS -$0.30, ROE -10.2%, ROIC -1.5%, NI -$30M FY25. The "profitability" is adjusted-EBITDA-and-recent-quarters, not durable trailing GAAP.
🔴 **Management itself flags the FY26 upside as partly transitory** ("in-year factors… better-than-expected Medicaid rates and favorable Medicare risk scores," Q3-26).
🔴 **2027 reimbursement compression looming** — V28 50/50 blended risk model, modest ~1.5-2% PACE rate, OBBBA/Medicaid state fiscal pressure, redeterminations. The exact macro that just *helped* can reverse.
🔴 **Relentless C-suite churn** — CMO (Feifer) out Q3-25; President/COO (Scarbrough) out Q1-26; new CMO/CAO inside two quarters. A transformation run by a revolving leadership bench.
🔴 **Valuation is rich, not cheap** — P/FCF 47x, ~12x FY26 adj. EBITDA, stock +42% vs 200d, 81% up its 52-wk range, analyst target $6.80 = **−27% downside.** The deep_value label is contradicted by every price-relative metric. The re-rate has largely happened.
🔴 **De novo Florida drag growing** ($13.4-15.4M FY26).

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **The Q3-26 pivot in word choice** is the single most important hidden signal: from "expanding margins" (FY25-Q2-26) to *"investing in growth while maintaining a consistent margin is probably more of a priority than expanding margins."* Translation: **the margin tailwind is topping**; don't model further expansion.
- **"In-year factors… more favorably than we expected"** — a CEO voluntarily attributing a beat to luck is a soft pre-announcement that the run-rate may not hold into FY27.
- **New CFO voice (Adams) on "P&L geography"** — repeated coaching of analysts to look at lines *together* because in-sourcing scrambles the comparisons. Honest, but it also means the reported organic trend is partially un-auditable from outside.
- **AI framing escalates each call** (Q4-25 "early days" → Q3-26 a full section led by the new CMO). This is *cost-takeout* dressed as innovation — scheduling/transportation/coordination efficiency. Reasonable, but it is being introduced precisely as the rate tailwind fades, i.e., it's the **next margin lever to replace the one running out.**
- **Voluntary disenrollment ~6-12%** disclosed for the first time (Q2-26) — a quiet admission of a retention leak the "participant experience" initiative is meant to plug.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Trajectory: STRENGTHENING (operationally) but with the easy gains LARGELY HARVESTED and a valuation that already prices them in.**

The single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing: **the load-bearing catalyst — the operational/margin turnaround — has substantially FIRED.** Adj. EBITDA went from $34.5M to a $85-90M run-rate, the stock tripled off its $3.13 low to ~$9.28, and it now trades RICH (47x P/FCF, ~12x fwd EBITDA, −27% to the $6.80 street target). The question is no longer "will the turnaround work" (it did) but "**is the FY26 margin level durable into a 2027 reimbursement-compression year that management itself is hedging?**" The asymmetry that existed at $3-5 is gone at $9; what remains is a still-GAAP-unprofitable, PE-controlled (Apax+WCAS ~70%+, a forced-seller overhang) capitated healthcare operator priced for continued execution into a softening rate backdrop.

**MOAT:** The durable advantage is a **regulatory-license + integrated-payer/provider scale** moat — PACE is a protected, capitated niche and INNV is the #1 operator. But returns are the evidence, and **ROIC is -1.5% and ROE -10.2%** on a trailing basis. A moat that does not yet earn above its cost of capital is, at best, **NARROW and only now inflecting** — not wide. The recent margin/ROIC trend is *improving* (widening from a low base), but the absolute return level is still sub-WACC.

**SECULAR THREAT:** **Reimbursement compression** (V28 risk-model transition, OBBBA/Medicaid directed-payment and provider-tax pressure, the 2028 ACA/Medicaid glide) is a real, dated structural force on the whole capitated-government-health cohort — and it is **MATERIAL, not terminal**: PACE's frailty adjuster and dual-eligible niche provide partial insulation, and the model is favored by MACPAC. It compresses the rate of the just-realized margin gain rather than impairing the franchise's existence. The cheapness-vs-quality read: this is **NOT** a fear-print mispricing (the stock is up, not down, and is rich) — it is a **fairly-to-fully-valued turnaround whose margin tailwind is at risk of fading**, with the value lever being a possible PE-sponsor monetization rather than further multiple expansion on fundamentals.

CREDIBILITY_SCORE: 4 | TRAJECTORY: STRENGTHENING | MOAT: NARROW | MOAT_TREND: WIDENING | SECULAR_THREAT: material

bull_thesis: InnovAge is the largest operator in the U.S. of PACE centers (the Program of All-inclusive Care for the Elderly, a model that combines the insurer and the care provider into one organization paid a fixed amount per member to handle all of a senior's medical needs). It serves about 8,050 frail, low-income seniors who qualify for both Medicare and Medicaid, across 20 centers in 6 states, and it is delivering a credible, fully corroborated operational turnaround after a 2021 sanction from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS). The core evidence is a genuine pattern of beating its own targets and then raising them. Guidance for adjusted EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization, stripped of one-off items) in fiscal 2026 was set at $56-65M and lifted twice over the year to $85-90M, on revenue of $950-975M. The 8-9% EBITDA margin goal management laid out at its Investor Day was reached in the second quarter of fiscal 2026 at 9.2% — about two years ahead of plan, which chief executive Patrick Blair described as pulling three years of progress into two. Revenue is compounding at a genuine double-digit pace (up 15% in the third quarter of fiscal 2026, and more than 10% a year over three years), the gross margin is structurally high and still expanding at 68.5% thanks to the fixed-payment PACE model, and the company posted its first positive net income under standard accounting since 2021 in the first quarter of fiscal 2026. The balance sheet has been paid down to roughly $45M of net cash (cash of $139M against debt of $94M), trailing-twelve-month free cash flow has turned positive at about $39M, and open-market insider activity shows real conviction (12 purchases totaling 181,653 shares versus 13 sales totaling 70,005 shares). Demand is rising over the long run and the model is independently endorsed: the Medicaid and CHIP Payment and Access Commission (MACPAC) named PACE the most fully integrated care model for people on both Medicare and Medicaid, citing roughly $6,000 a year in Medicaid savings per person versus nursing homes. The longer-term upside is expansion into Medicare-only PACE and PACE-adjacent products, which management is actively advocating with CMS and its innovation arm. If fiscal 2027 rate pressure proves, in management's words, a near-term dynamic rather than a structural shift, and if efforts to improve enrollment and cut the 10-12% rate of members voluntarily leaving carry the line, this becomes a durable-margin franchise that compounds and the valuation holds up.
bear_thesis: The turnaround is real, but it is finished and now fully reflected in the price — the event that was supposed to drive the stock has already happened. The shares have run from a 52-week low of about $3.13 to $8.97 (within 77% of the 52-week range, and 36% above the 200-day moving average), and the margin re-rating that powered that move is spent: management itself says it has pulled three years of progress into two, has hit the 8-9% margin goal, and has explicitly shifted its priority toward growth and reinvestment rather than further margin gains. At about 45 times price-to-free-cash-flow and roughly 13-14 times enterprise value to the raised adjusted EBITDA, with negative returns on invested capital (-1.5%), negative return on equity (-10.2%), and still losing money under standard accounting (a net loss of $30M in fiscal 2025, and a $29.9M loss in the third quarter of fiscal 2026 alone on $51.9M of litigation), the name looks expensive next to profitable senior-care peers — Ensign Group earns about 15% on invested capital, and Encompass Health and Amedisys are profitable — which matches the peer read that it is richly valued versus comparable companies. The litigation tail is still draining cash: a $27M securities-fraud class-action settled in December 2025, a $16.3M Grane Rx vendor settlement in March 2026, and $34.4M of fresh accruals in the third quarter of fiscal 2026. The decisive forward risk is reimbursement compression: management flagged fiscal 2027 Medicare PACE rates at only +1.5-2% and muted Medicaid rates because of state-budget pressure — meaning the next 12 months carry the risk of downward rate revisions, not a fresh upside trigger, for a business whose above-cost-of-capital returns are pure policy rent. The fiscal 2026 upside was partly one-time (better-than-expected Medicaid rates and favorable Medicare risk scores, by management's own account), so the EBITDA run-rate may not last. Add the departures of the chief medical officer and the chief operating officer/president within a single year of transformation, widening losses on newly opened centers ($13-15M in fiscal 2026), and an artificial-intelligence and quality narrative that replaces a spent catalyst with an unprovable one. The analyst price target is $6.80 — roughly 24% below the current price.
consensus_delta: For a deep-value name the test is whether the market is pricing terminal decline into a company that actually has stable or growing earnings power. Here the answer is no — the market is correctly pricing a completed turnaround, and if anything it may still be slightly too generous. The sell-side consensus target is $6.80, roughly 24% below the $8.97 share price, with a hold consensus (no buy ratings and two holds). Where the market's expectations are arguably wrong, they run against the bull case on the forward view: first, the raised fiscal 2026 adjusted EBITDA guidance of $85-90M embeds management's own disclosed one-time tailwinds (better-than-expected Medicaid rates and favorable Medicare risk scores) that will not repeat every year, so the run-rate the market is extrapolating may be too high; second, management itself flagged fiscal 2027 Medicare PACE rates at only +1.5-2% and muted Medicaid, meaning the risk of downward rate revisions that momentum buyers chasing a 186% move off the low are under-weighting. There is no hidden, mispriced-cheap thesis here: price-to-free-cash-flow is 45.6 times, enterprise value to EBITDA is in the mid-teens, returns on invested capital are negative, the company loses money under standard accounting, and it sits near a 52-week high. The gap between expectations and reality is the opposite of a deep-value setup — the apparent cheapness on enterprise value to gross profit (about 1.8 times) is an artifact of the PACE cost structure, not a real margin of safety.
forcing_function: The September 2026 release of fiscal 2027 guidance, alongside the fiscal 2026 fourth-quarter and full-year results, is the hard-dated event most likely to force the market to re-price the stock — but it points the wrong way for anyone who is long. Management has explicitly set fiscal 2027 up as the moment of truth on rates (in their words, they will have more to say once they get through the budget process and issue guidance in September), with Medicare PACE rates of +1.5-2% and muted Medicaid already flagged. This is a re-rating trigger, but the asymmetry points down: guidance that confirms rate compression or flat-to-lower margins would break the continuation story. There is no upside-forcing event in the window — no buyback authorization, no asset sale, no activist investor, no dividend, no pending deal. The one long-term upside, expansion into Medicare-only PACE, is policy-dependent and has no date attached. The catalyst that the whole deep-value thesis rested on — the margin inflection — has already happened.
valley_of_death: Three near-term hazards (over the next 3-9 months) land before any fresh catalyst could. First, fiscal 2027 guidance, due in September 2026, roughly one quarter out: management has pre-flagged a more restrained backdrop for Medicare and Medicaid rates and has deflected every analyst question about fiscal 2027 to that September print — the single most likely negative surprise, into a stock sitting at 77% of its 52-week range. A muted fiscal 2027 EBITDA-growth or flat-margin guide would puncture the continuation thesis. Second, the roll-off of one-time tailwinds: the fiscal 2026 beat was driven partly by favorable Medicaid rates and Medicare risk scores that management explicitly called in-year factors that came in better than expected — these wash out and pressure the fourth-quarter fiscal 2026 and fiscal 2027 run-rate. Third, the litigation and accounting drag persists: $51.9M of litigation hit the third quarter of fiscal 2026 (including $34.4M of accruals and the $16.3M Grane Rx settlement); the $27M securities settlement closed in December 2025, but the accounting loss and any residual accruals keep the headline net result negative, which is what drove the 7.3% drop after the print in May 2026 despite the guidance raise. Widening losses on newly opened Florida centers (to $13-15M in fiscal 2026) add a fourth, smaller drag. None of these is a solvency event.
positioning_washout: There is no classic forced-seller dynamic here. InnovAge pays no dividend, so there is no yield-focused base that a cut could flush out, and at roughly $1.2B in market value it is a small-to-mid-cap with thin analyst coverage (two analysts). The relevant mechanical risk is the opposite of buying into a wash-out: the shareholder base is now made up of turnaround and momentum money that bought the 186% move off the $3.13 low, and that base is weak hands if the fiscal 2027 guide disappoints, with no value-buyer floor underneath because the stock is expensive versus peers and the sell-side target ($6.80) sits below the current price. A smart-money score of 56 out of 100 and an institutional-flow score of 48 out of 100 are middling — no strong sign of accumulation at these levels. So rather than a capitulation flush that creates an entry, the risk is a momentum-driven unwind on the September fiscal 2027 print, with limited fundamental support until roughly $6-7.
moderator_conclusion: Our recommendation is to pass for now, with low conviction. InnovAge is a genuine, well-executed PACE turnaround — and that is precisely the problem: the thesis worked, the re-rating has already happened, and the stock now trades at about $8.97 (up 186% from its $3.13 low, 77% of the way up its 52-week range) at roughly 45 times price-to-free-cash-flow and mid-teens enterprise value to adjusted EBITDA, with negative returns on invested capital and a base that still loses money under standard accounting. The peer read — that it is richly valued versus comparable companies — is correct, and, importantly, the gap here reflects sector-wide regulatory sensitivity (a shared Medicaid and Medicare rate driver across the government-funded health cohort), not name-specific advantage. Under the current screening stance (the catalyst-watch lens adopted on 2026-06-05), the system rewards hard-dated catalysts inside a favorable window and penalizes ones that have already fired or fallen out of the window, along with theses that hinge on a single shared macro factor. InnovAge fails on both counts — its catalyst has already fired, and its forward is governed by one shared, unresolved factor (fiscal 2027 reimbursement rates) skewed to the downside (management has pre-flagged compression), with the only dated event (the September 2026 fiscal 2027 guide) more likely to break the thesis than confirm it. The stance also deprioritizes reimbursement-compression names, and InnovAge is the archetype. Risk and reward run about one-to-two against the long position (downside to the bear case is roughly $6.0, or -33%, versus upside to the bull case of about $10.5, or +17%), and base-case fair value of about $7.00 is roughly 28% below the current price, corroborated by the $6.80 sell-side target. The competitive moat is narrow (built on its regulatory license, scale, and leading PACE census) and widening operationally, but it is a policy-rent moat at the top of its widening cycle heading into a flagged rate down-cycle. Setting the catalyst and screening stance aside entirely, the value case on its own is also weak: on valuation versus a sum-of-the-parts estimate (valuing the business on its own merits), the stock is expensive, not cheap — fair value of about $7 against the $8.97 price, 45 times price-to-free-cash-flow, negative returns on invested capital, accounting losses, and a peer-confirmed rich read. The apparent cheapness on enterprise value to gross profit (about 1.8 times) is an artifact of the PACE cost structure, not a margin of safety. The forensic quality is genuinely good (a high credibility read, strong guidance reliability, net cash, insider buying) — which is why the value case rates weak rather than the worst score — but a fair-to-rich price on a name with material exposure to reimbursement compression is not a value entry. No position.
sop_breakdown: A single PACE operating segment, valued for the whole company on normalized adjusted EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization, stripped of one-off items). Base case: about $78M of normalized adjusted EBITDA — the raised fiscal 2026 guidance midpoint of about $87.5M, cut back for management's own disclosed one-time fiscal 2026 tailwinds (better-than-expected Medicaid rates and favorable Medicare risk scores) and for fiscal 2027 rate compression — multiplied by about 12.5 times enterprise value to adjusted EBITDA. That multiple is a slight premium to rate-exposed but profitable senior-care peers (Ensign Group, Encompass Health, Amedisys), reflecting InnovAge's scale-leading PACE niche and the rising-demand tailwind, but it sits below the bull case's 16 times because the company still loses money under standard accounting and its return on invested capital is negative. That gives an enterprise value of about $975M. Add roughly $45M of net cash (a genuine add-back: cash of $139M against debt of $94M, recomputed from the balance sheet). Subtract an implicit haircut already built in for the residual litigation tail. The result is about $1.02B of equity divided by roughly 136M shares, or about $7.5 a share, rounded down to a $7.00 anchor to reflect the downward skew in fiscal 2027 rate revisions and the peer-confirmed rich read. Enterprise value to gross profit (the screen's headline metric) is about 1.8 times on roughly $650M of fiscal 2026 gross profit, but that is a misleading lens here: PACE gross profit sits on top of a care-coordination and overhead base that consumes nearly all of it (the net margin is -3.6%), so enterprise value to gross profit overstates the cheapness — the EBITDA and free-cash-flow lenses are the ones that govern.
risk_reward: Poor, ranging from balanced to negative. Upside to fair value: at a $8.97 share price against a base-case fair value of about $7.00, the stock is roughly 28% above what we think it is intrinsically worth. Upside to the bull-case sum-of-the-parts of about $10.5 is +17%; downside to the bear-case sum-of-the-parts of about $6.0 is -33%. That is roughly one-to-two reward-to-risk against the long position. The analyst consensus target of $6.80 sits below the current price. This is a successful turnaround priced for continuation, not a discounted recovery.
peer_comps_note: The peer read is that InnovAge is richly valued versus comparable companies, and that is the correct call. Its closest comparables are senior and post-acute care operators — Ensign Group (about 15% return on invested capital), Encompass Health and Amedisys (both profitable), and Select Medical. InnovAge runs a negative return on invested capital (-1.5%) at about 45 times price-to-free-cash-flow and roughly 13-14 times enterprise value to adjusted EBITDA on the raised guidance — expensive on every current-earnings measure versus profitable peers. One critical point on where the gap comes from: it is a sector-wide regulatory gap. InnovAge, the hospital group (Tenet Healthcare and Universal Health Services), and other government-funded health names all move on the same unresolved driver (Medicaid directed payments, the state-budget pressure of the 2025 budget reconciliation era, and the normalization of Medicare risk scores). So InnovAge's policy-rent discount or premium is shared-factor sector sensitivity, not a company-specific edge; the fiscal 2027 rate compression management flagged is a cohort-wide event, not a name-specific advantage. We should not credit the PACE niche premium as durable advantage — it is a regulatory-rent multiple that falls as the whole cohort de-rates. No stale prior multiple is cited here; the peer reads are current and rate-exposed.
interrogator_dossier:
# InnovAge Holding Corp. (INNV) — A Deep Dive on Management and the Setup
*PACE (Program of All-inclusive Care for the Elderly) operator · Healthcare · deep-value signal · 6 earnings-call transcripts, third quarter of fiscal 2025 through third quarter of fiscal 2026 (May 2025 to May 2026)*

## 1. The story across the calls

The story across these six calls is a textbook operational-turnaround arc that has now largely completed in front of investors' eyes — and management's language tracks that completion almost too neatly.

- **Third quarter of fiscal 2025 (May 2025) — from stabilization to transformation.** Chief executive Patrick Blair frames the company as having shifted from operational stabilization to enterprise transformation. Revenue was $218M, adjusted EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization, stripped of one-off items) was $10.8M (a 4.9% margin), and census was about 7,530. The emphasis is on fixing things: clearing the California enrollment backlog, bringing down losses on newly opened centers ($3.5M a quarter), and bringing hospice and pharmacy in-house. This is a company still digging out of the 2021 sanction from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) and the securities-fraud overhang.
- **Fourth quarter of fiscal 2025 (September 2025) — a year of delivery.** Adjusted EBITDA was $34.5M for fiscal 2025, above the high end of the $31M guidance; the margin nearly doubled from 2.2% to 4%. Fiscal 2026 guidance was set at $900-950M of revenue and $56-65M of adjusted EBITDA. The narrative pivots from repair to delivering on commitments (we committed to this, and we delivered) — a confidence inflection.
- **First quarter of fiscal 2026 (November 2025) — first positive net income since 2021.** Revenue was $236M (up 15%) and adjusted EBITDA was $17.6M. Blair's reflection on his fourth anniversary cements the turnaround framing. New growth vectors are introduced: advocacy for Medicare-only PACE expansion, and development of PACE-adjacent products.
- **Second quarter of fiscal 2026 (February 2026) — the margin target is hit.** The adjusted EBITDA margin reached 9.2% — the 8-9% Investor-Day target, for the first time — and first-half adjusted EBITDA ($39.8M) already exceeded all of fiscal 2025. This is the catalyst firing in real time.
- **Third quarter of fiscal 2026 (May 2026) — guidance raised again, but caution about fiscal 2027 enters.** Revenue was $252M, adjusted EBITDA was $30M (about a 9.2% margin), and census was about 8,050. Fiscal 2026 guidance was raised twice over the year to $950-975M of revenue and $85-90M of adjusted EBITDA (against the $56-65M starting point — a striking pattern of beating targets and raising them). But a new defensive note appears: management is being thoughtful about its assumptions heading into fiscal 2027, flagging a more restrained Medicare and Medicaid rate backdrop. Artificial-intelligence investment language expands (clinical AI led by chief medical officer Taheri). The priority explicitly shifts toward growth and reinvestment over further margin expansion.

**Drivers introduced and quietly re-weighted:** the early calls leaned on cost discipline and medical-cost management; by the third quarter of fiscal 2026 the durable-margin story is intact but management openly says it has pulled three years of progress into two — meaning the easy margin gains are behind it, and the forward story now rests on census growth, retention (cutting the roughly 10-12% rate of members voluntarily leaving), and unproven AI efficiency.

## 2. Claims versus financials

| Management claim | Transcript evidence | Hard-number check | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Durable, accelerating profitability" | Adjusted EBITDA $10.8M → $17.6M → $22.2M → $30M across the run | Screener: net income still negative every fiscal year 2021-2025 (net loss of $30M in fiscal 2025, earnings per share of -$0.22); return on equity -10.2%, return on invested capital -1.5% | Diverges sharply. The improvement is real on adjusted EBITDA, but the company still loses money under standard accounting. The gap between adjusted EBITDA and the standard-accounting result is wide (litigation, losses on newly opened centers, stock-based compensation). |
| "Revenue growth above 10% a year" | Fiscal 2023 to fiscal 2025 above 10% a year; third quarter of fiscal 2026 up 15% | Screener: revenue $638M → $699M → $688M → $764M → $854M; up 11.8% year over year, 6.9% a year over three years | Matches. The top line is genuinely growing double digits; this claim holds up. |
| "Strong medical-cost management versus peers under pressure" | Cost per member per month declining each quarter; a closed-loop model | Gross margin 68.5% and expanding — unusually high, reflecting the fixed-payment PACE structure | Matches in direction. The gross margin is a real structural feature, not spin. |
| "Cash flow generation gives strategic flexibility" | More consistent earnings and cash flow (third quarter of fiscal 2026) | Trailing-twelve-month free cash flow $39M, free-cash-flow margin 3.1%, net cash of about $45M (cash $139M against debt $94M) | Mostly supported — the balance sheet is genuinely de-levered and free cash flow turned positive. But 45.6 times price-to-free-cash-flow says the market already prices the inflection. |
| "Clean compliance, no material deficiencies" | Fourth quarter of fiscal 2025: no material compliance deficiencies | But the third quarter of fiscal 2026 widened the standard-accounting loss to -$29.9M on $51.9M of litigation ($34.4M of accruals plus the $16.3M Grane Rx vendor settlement); a $27M securities-fraud class action settled in December 2025 | Poorer than narrated. Compliance is clean going forward, but the legal tail from the 2021-era sanction is still draining cash as of the third quarter of fiscal 2026. |
; "Fiscal 2026 guidance"; Set at $56-65M of adjusted EBITDA, raised to $85-90M; Independently confirmed (StockTitan and Seeking Alpha, May 2026); Over-delivered massively — the single strongest credibility marker here.; 

**Net read:** the narrative is richer than the standard-accounting results justify, but poorer than the headline adjusted EBITDA implies once litigation and the losses on newly opened centers are netted out. The honest read: a real operational turnaround sitting on top of a base that still loses money under standard accounting, with a live litigation tail.

## 3. Tone and confidence over time

**Rising, then subtly hedging at the top.**

- Third quarter of fiscal 2025: cautious and constructive — growing confidence, methodical, clear-eyed.
- Fourth quarter of fiscal 2025: confident and declarative — a year of delivery, we committed and we delivered (repeated as a refrain).
- First quarter of fiscal 2026: peak offensive — positive net income for the first time since 2021, the largest PACE provider by census.
- Second quarter of fiscal 2026: triumphal but careful — hitting the 8-9% target for the first time, yet immediately caveating that 9.2% is consistent with what is required to operate sustainably (managing down the expectation of further expansion).
- Third quarter of fiscal 2026: the first defensive pivot — being thoughtful about assumptions heading into fiscal 2027, a more restrained rate backdrop, and an explicit re-prioritization toward growth and reinvestment over margin expansion. Heavy new artificial-intelligence rhetoric reads as a reach for a forward narrative to keep the valuation alive now that the margin catalyst has fired.

Guidance grew more specific over the run (precise census, member-month, and EBITDA ranges by fiscal 2026), which is a credibility positive — but the third-quarter-of-fiscal-2026 deflection on fiscal 2027 (we will have more to say when we issue guidance in September) is the tell that the easy chapter is closing.

## 4. Guidance credibility

**Read: high (this is the strongest part of the bull case).**

- Fiscal 2025 adjusted EBITDA guided at about $31M, delivered $34.5M (a beat).
- Fiscal 2026 guided at $56-65M, then raised to $71M and higher, then $85-90M over three successive prints. A genuine, repeated pattern of beating and raising.
- The screener beat-rate of 3 out of 6 (50%) understates this because it measures standard-accounting earnings per share (still negative), not the adjusted EBITDA path that management steers to.

The one pattern that edges toward sandbagging: chief financial officer Ben Adams repeatedly declined to commit on the implied fourth quarter (in the third quarter of fiscal 2025: we are happy to let guidance stand) and then beat — consistent conservatism, not evasion. Reliability is high on adjusted EBITDA and low on standard-accounting earnings per share (which the company does not guide and which keeps missing on litigation).

## 5. Where analysts pushed

- **Losses on newly opened centers, especially Florida** (Goldman's Perse, repeatedly): management consistently says it is tracking to expectations but admits it took a little longer to get out of the gate, with transportation cost overruns in Florida. These losses are guided to widen to $13.4-15.4M in fiscal 2026 — a real drag analysts keep circling.
- **How costs are categorized** (Gillmore, JPMorgan): bringing pharmacy in-house scrambled the income statement; cost of care rose 19.7% quarter over quarter in the first quarter of fiscal 2026 and looked alarming, requiring management to point out that you do not have all the pieces to reconcile it. This is an opacity risk — the model transition makes quarter-over-quarter trend reads unreliable, which analysts flagged.
- **The fiscal 2027 rate setup** (Haase, Rossi): analysts pushed directly on the growth-versus-margin trade-off and the acuity and risk-score mix for fiscal 2027; management deflected to the September guide. This is the topic most likely to turn into bad news.
- **Members voluntarily leaving** (about 10-12% a year, or 6% on Blair's annualized framing): a recurring retention drag management is only now starting to measure.

## 6. Warning signs and positives

Warning signs:
- Still losing money under standard accounting (a net loss of $30M in fiscal 2025; -$29.9M in the third quarter of fiscal 2026 alone on litigation) despite the adjusted EBITDA story — the gap is wide.
- The litigation tail is still draining cash as of the third quarter of fiscal 2026 ($27M securities settlement in December 2025, plus $16.3M Grane Rx and $34.4M of accruals) — the legacy of the 2021 sanction is not fully closed.
- The fiscal 2027 rate headwind is flagged by management itself — Medicare PACE rates of +1.5-2% (modest) and muted Medicaid on state-budget pressure; this is the core reimbursement-compression secular risk.
- The margin catalyst has fired — the 8-9% target is hit, and management says it has pulled three years of progress into two, meaning the fuel for the re-rating is spent.
- Losses on newly opened centers are widening, to $13-15M in fiscal 2026.

Positives:
- A genuine pattern of beating targets and raising them — the adjusted EBITDA guide went from $56-65M to $85-90M in one year.
- A net-cash balance sheet of about $45M (cash $139M against debt $94M), with trailing-twelve-month free cash flow positive at about $39M.
- Real insider conviction (open-market only: 12 purchases of 181,653 shares versus 13 sales of 70,005 shares — net buying).
- A structurally high gross margin (68.5%, expanding) and the largest PACE census in the U.S. — a niche built on regulatory license and scale.
- A MACPAC endorsement of PACE as the most integrated model for people on both Medicare and Medicaid — supporting the long-term demand case.

## 7. Signals beneath the surface

- **Heavy leadership churn, quietly disclosed:** the chief medical officer Feifer left (third quarter of fiscal 2025), then the chief operating officer and president Scarbrough departed for personal reasons (first quarter of fiscal 2026) just as a new chief medical officer (Taheri), chief administrative officer (Delk), and an expanded chief growth officer role (Huray) arrived. Management frames it as bench depth, but losing both a chief medical officer and the operating chief operating officer within a year, mid-transformation, is a real continuity question dressed up as routine.
- **A vocabulary shift from cost discipline to reinvestment, quality, and AI:** by the third quarter of fiscal 2026 the language moves off the margin lever (now maxed out) onto softer, longer-dated value claims (participant experience, retention, AI-enabled scheduling). This is the narrative replacing a spent catalyst with an unprovable one.
- **A spans-and-layers review** — a reduction in corporate shared-services headcount, a late-cycle efficiency move consistent with margins having peaked.
- **Medicare-only PACE and adjacency advocacy** is introduced as a growth vector — entirely policy-dependent and speculative; a tell that organic census growth alone may not carry the valuation.

## 8. Capital-allocation verdict (for the director)

**Classification: stable, tilting toward turning.** The operational turnaround is real and credible, but it is complete: the margin catalyst that drove the stock from about $3 to about $9 (a 186% move to within 77% of the 52-week range) has fired, and management has openly shifted from margin expansion to growth and reinvestment while flagging a fiscal 2027 rate headwind. The single most important thing the director must weigh: this is a successful turnaround being priced as if the turnaround were still ahead — at about 45 times price-to-free-cash-flow and roughly 14 times enterprise value to the raised adjusted EBITDA, the cheapness is gone, and the next 12 months carry the risk of downward rate revisions (fiscal 2027 Medicaid and Medicare compression) rather than a fresh upside catalyst.

**Economic moat: narrow, built on regulatory license, scale, and cost** (the largest PACE census, a fixed-payment structure, and high switching costs for frail enrolled seniors). It is widening operationally (return on invested capital moving from deeply negative toward break-even, margins expanded to 9.2%) — but it remains a policy-rent business: returns exceed the cost of capital only when Medicaid directed payments and Medicare PACE rates cooperate, and both are now flagged to compress. So the moat is narrow and effectively at the top of its widening cycle.

**Secular threat: material — reimbursement compression** (the exact archetype: Medicaid re-determinations, the state-budget pressure of the 2025 budget reconciliation era, the normalization of Medicare risk scores and coding intensity, and the fiscal 2027 rate lag management itself disclosed). This is not terminal — PACE demand is rising over the long run and the model is endorsed (MACPAC) — but it caps the long-term value: a low-single-digit free-cash-flow margin on a base whose reimbursement is being squeezed is a fear-and-recovery story that has already played out, not a forward mispricing. The cheapness is spent, not present.

Credibility read: high. Trajectory: stable. Moat: narrow but real. Moat trend: widening. Secular threat: material.
